name: Ahlström, Christer
recent_1: Ahlström, C., Kircher, K., Johansson, F., Andersson, A., & Olstam, J. (2025). Driver attention in urban intersections when crossing paths with cyclists. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 223, 108276. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2025.108276
recent_1_synopsis: A driving simulator study with 44 drivers examined whether drivers adequately check for cyclists when turning across cycle paths in urban intersections. Participants failed to look for cyclists approaching from behind in nearly half of the encounters, and many were unaware of their legal obligation to do so. The work links glance behavior and attentional strategy to vulnerable-road-user safety.
recent_2: Ahlström, C., & Anund, A. (2025). Development of sleepiness in professional truck drivers: Real-road testing for driver drowsiness and attention warning (DDAW) system evaluation. Journal of Sleep Research, 34(2), e14259. DOI: 10.1111/jsr.14259
recent_2_synopsis: Twenty-four professional truck drivers drove 180 km on a motorway during daytime (after normal sleep) and at night (after extended wakefulness) to establish a test procedure for validating Driver Drowsiness and Attention Warning (DDAW) systems. Sleepiness increased markedly at night and intensified with distance driven. The study supports regulatory validation protocols for in-vehicle drowsiness warning systems.
recent_3: Ahlström, C., Kircher, K., Nyström, M., & Wolfe, B. (2021). A Review of Occlusion as a Tool to Assess Attentional Demand in Driving. Human Factors, 63(8), 1306-1326. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211010953
recent_3_synopsis: This review synthesizes the visual occlusion technique, in which drivers' vision is intermittently blocked, as a method for quantifying the attentional demand of driving and secondary tasks. It catalogs how occlusion has been applied across simulator and on-road studies and discusses methodological choices, strengths, and limitations. The paper consolidates occlusion as a measure of how much visual attention different driving situations require.
seminal_1: Barua, S., Ahmed, M. U., Ahlström, C., & Begum, S. (2019). Automatic driver sleepiness detection using EEG, EOG and contextual information. Expert Systems with Applications, 115, 121-135. DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2018.07.054
seminal_1_synopsis: Using data from 30 drivers tested in a high-fidelity simulator in both alert and sleep-deprived states, this paper builds automatic sleepiness classifiers (k-NN, SVM, case-based reasoning, random forest) from EEG, EOG, and contextual signals, finding steering-wheel position and light condition to be strong contextual predictors. It is highly cited (approximately 181 citations, Google Scholar June 2026) and is foundational because it operationalizes multimodal physiological sleepiness detection with rigorous machine-learning evaluation, a template widely adopted in driver-monitoring research.
seminal_2: Kircher, K., & Ahlström, C. (2017). Minimum Required Attention: A Human-Centered Approach to Driver Inattention. Human Factors, 59(3), 471-484. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816672756
seminal_2_synopsis: This conceptual paper introduces the Minimum Required Attention (MiRA) theory, defining driver inattention not as time looking away but as failure to sample the information the current situation actually demands, treating driving as a satisficing, partly self-paced task. With approximately 172 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is foundational for reframing the very definition of driver inattention and underpins much of Ahlström's subsequent occlusion and attentional-demand work.
expertise_overview: Christer Ahlström's research centers on driver attention, distraction, and inattention, and on driver drowsiness, sleepiness, and fatigue, studied primarily in the transportation and road-safety domain. Methodologically he combines physiological signal processing (EEG, EOG, ECG/heart-rate variability), eye tracking and glance/occlusion analysis, and machine-learning classification to detect impaired driver states. He frequently uses driving simulators and naturalistic/real-road on-road studies, including work with professional truck drivers and partially automated (Level 2) driving. He also develops conceptual frameworks such as Minimum Required Attention that redefine what counts as adequate driver attention. Recent threads extend to vulnerable-road-user (cyclist) interactions, alcohol and drug impairment detection via eye tracking, train-driver visual behavior, and validation protocols for driver-monitoring and drowsiness-warning (DDAW) systems. His underlying disciplinary base is biomedical engineering and biomedical signal processing.
notes: Confidently identified (found=yes). This is Christer Ahlström, Senior Researcher at VTI (Swedish National Road and Transport Research Institute), Linköping, and Associate Professor in biomedical signal processing at Linköping University; ORCID 0000-0003-4134-0303. Disambiguation confirmed against the four seed hf_papers (all on driver attention/occlusion/automated driving) and via Google Scholar (user KT6OEVwAAAAJ), VTI/Linköping employee pages, and ResearchGate. All six cited DOIs were verified to resolve via the Crossref API with matching titles (checked June 2026); the four seed DOIs were pre-verified. Citation counts for the two seminal papers (~181 and ~172) are from Google Scholar profile, retrieved June 2026, and are approximate and subject to change; Scholar overall metrics at that time: total ~4,971 citations, h-index 41. No citations are unverifiable. Note: an alternative most-cited candidate, 'The impact of tunnel design and lighting...' (Accident Analysis & Prevention, 2012, ~240 citations), was not chosen as seminal because the two selected papers are more central to his core driver-monitoring/attention expertise; both selected seminal DOIs nonetheless verify cleanly.
